quick_test: Can you say 1 ___, 2 ___? If YES → How many. If NO → How much.
word_order: colour + noun: a red shirt, blue jeans, a black jacket
interesting_facts: Dolphins can communicate with each other.
Owls can see in the dark.
Peacocks can open their feathers like a fan.
Tigers can't purr but lions can roar very loudly.

connectors.example: First, we took the train to Ooty.
Then we checked into our hotel.
Next, we visited the tea gardens.
Later in the afternoon, we went shopping.
Finally, we took a long train ride home.

usage.use: State, description, feeling at that time

example_story_sentence: The day was sunny (was), so we went to the beach (irregular), ate fresh fish (irregular), and walked for hours (regular). It was the best day of the trip (was)!
vs_simple_past: Simple past: happened once. Used to: happened regularly/continuously in the past.
important: The past form is the SAME for all subjects — I worked, She worked, They worked.

common_error.right: She didn't work. (base verb after didn't)

contrast_pattern.explanation: Very useful: say what you DID and what you DIDN'T do in the same sentence.
examples: I went to work but I didn't finish my report.
She cooked dinner but she didn't wash the dishes.
We went to the market but we didn't buy anything.

questions.example: What did you eat for lunch?
How long did the journey take?

title: Must — strong obligation
body:
We use MUST + base verb to say something is necessary or very important.

Examples:
• You must wear a helmet on a bike.
• Students must carry their ID cards.
• I must call my mother today.

MUST shows a strong personal or official rule. It often comes from the speaker or from authority.

vocabulary.meaning: money you pay for breaking a rule
vocabulary.example: You will get a fine for speeding.
Parking there is a traffic violation.

signs.meaning: You mustn't go in. Entry is prohibited.
You must wear a hard hat here.
Use only in emergency. You mustn't block this exit.
Be careful — the floor is slippery. Walk slowly.
Very dangerous electricity — do not touch.
Come here in case of fire or emergency.

content:
Here are typical problem areas where advice is given or asked for:

🏥 HEALTH: headache, stress, lack of sleep, weight, diet
💰 MONEY: overspending, saving, salary, loans
✈️ TRAVEL: visa, packing, delays, being lost
📚 STUDY: exams, procrastination, concentration, motivation
💼 WORK: conflicts with colleagues, workload, career choices
🏠 HOME: managing responsibilities, living alone for the first time

Knowing the vocabulary for each area helps you give relevant, helpful advice.
